questions.text: Which kind of therapy was given in 1990 to a four­ year­ girl with Adenosine Deaminase (ADA) enzyme deficiency?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In 1990, a four-year-old girl with ADA deficiency was the first patient to receive gene therapy. This groundbreaking treatment involved introducing functional ADA genes into the patient's cells to correct the genetic defect causing the deficiency.
Chemotherapy is a treatment that uses drugs to kill or inhibit the growth of rapidly dividing cells, such as cancer cells. It is not a treatment for ADA deficiency.

Immunotherapy involves using the immune system to treat diseases. While it has been explored as a treatment for various conditions, it is not the primary therapy for adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency.
Radiation therapy is a treatment that uses high-energy radiation to target and kill cancer cells. It is not a treatment for ADA deficiency.

questions.explanation: Gene therapy is a technique of genetic engineering which involves the replacement of a faulty/disease-causing gene by a normal healthy functional gene. The first clinical ex-vivo gene therapy was given in 1990 to a 4­-year old girl with adenosine deaminase (ADA) enzyme deficiency. This enzyme is very important for the immune system to function and the deficiency of this enzyme can lead to severe combined immune deficiency (SCID).

questions.text: Which part of the tobacco plant is infected by Meloidogyne incognita?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While nematodes like Meloidogyne incognita can potentially cause damage to plant stems indirectly by affecting root health, they are not typically considered stem-infecting pathogens. Their primary target is the root system.

Meloidogyne incognita, commonly known as the root-knot nematode, infects the roots of various plant species, including tobacco. The nematode causes characteristic galls or swellings on the roots, which can lead to stunted growth and reduced crop yield. So, in the case of tobacco plants, it is the roots that are infected by Meloidogyne incognita.
Meloidogyne incognita is a nematode species that primarily infects the roots of various host plants. It does not typically infect the flowers.
Meloidogyne incognita does not primarily infect the leaves of tobacco plants. Their primary site of infection is the root system.

questions.explanation: Meloidogyne incognita, commonly known as the root-knot nematode, infects the roots of various plant species, including tobacco. The nematode causes characteristic galls or swellings on the roots, which can lead to stunted growth and reduced crop yield. So, in the case of tobacco plants, it is the roots that are infected by Meloidogyne incognita.

questions.text: Golden rice is a genetically modified crop plant where the incorporated gene is meant for the biosynthesis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golden rice is not genetically modified to produce Omega-3 fatty acids. Its primary focus is on enhancing Vitamin A content.

Golden rice is a genetically modified crop plant designed to biosynthesize beta-carotene, which is a precursor of vitamin A. The incorporated gene in golden rice is responsible for the production of beta-carotene, a pigment that gives the rice its characteristic golden color. This genetic modification was introduced to address vitamin A deficiency in populations that rely heavily on rice as a staple food, as beta-carotene is converted into vitamin A in the human body. This fortification with beta-carotene is intended to improve the nutritional value of rice and combat vitamin A deficiency in regions where it is a significant health concern.
Golden rice is not genetically modified for the biosynthesis of Vitamin B. Its main objective is to increase Vitamin A content, which is a different vitamin.
Golden rice is not genetically modified to produce Vitamin C. It is engineered to produce a different vitamin.

questions.explanation: Golden rice is a transgenic variety of rice (Oryza sativa) which contains good quantities of B­-carotene (provitamin A - inactive state of vitamin A). B­-carotene is a principal source of vitamin A. The grains of this rice are yellow in color due to B­-carotene and are commonly called golden rice.

questions.text: The introduction of t-­DNA into plants involves:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Exposing the plants to cold for a brief period
options.explanation: Exposing plants to cold is not a standard method for introducing t-DNA into plants. The method typically involves genetic engineering techniques rather than temperature exposure.

options.text: Allowing the plant roots to stand in water
options.explanation: Introducing t-DNA into plants does not typically involve allowing plant roots to stand in water. The process of introducing t-DNA is different.

options.text: Infection of the plant by Agrobacterium tumefaciens
options.explanation: The introduction of t-DNA into plants involves infection of the plant by Agrobacterium tumefaciens.

options.text: Altering the pH of the soil and then heat­ shocking the plants.
options.explanation: This option is not a typical method for introducing t-DNA into plants. The process usually involves genetic modification techniques, not altering soil pH or heat-shocking plants.

questions.explanation: Ti plasmid (tumor inducing) from the soil bacterium Agrobacterium tumefaciens is effectively used as a vector for gene transfer to plant cells. The part of Ti plasmid transferred into plant cell DNA is called the T­-DNA. This T-­DNA, with desired DNA spliced into it, is inserted into the chromosomes of the host plant where it produces copies of itself, by migrating from one chromosomal position to another at random. Such plant cells are then cultured, induced to multiply, and then differentiate to form plantlets. Transferred into the soil, the plantlets grow into mature plants, carrying the foreign gene, expressing throughout the new plant.

questions.text: In Bt cotton, the Bt toxin present in plant tissue as protoxin is converted into active toxin due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While gut microorganisms can play a role in the breakdown of some substances in the insect gut, the primary mechanism for Bt toxin activation in Bt cotton is the alkaline pH of the insect gut, not the action of microorganisms.

options.text: Presence of conversion factors in the insect gut
options.explanation: The activation of Bt toxin in the insect gut does not typically involve conversion factors. It primarily relies on environmental conditions and specific gut factors.

options.text: Alkaline pH of the insect gut
options.explanation: Under these alkaline conditions, enzymes in the insect's gut cleave the Bt protoxin, removing a portion of it. This cleavage process converts the Bt protoxin into its active toxin form. The active toxin can then bind to specific receptors in the insect's gut lining.

options.text: Acidic pH of the insect gut
options.explanation: An acidic pH is not typically involved in the activation of Bt toxin. It is the alkaline pH that is important for this process.

questions.explanation: Soil bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis produces proteins that kill certain insects like lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles) and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms some protein crystals which contain a toxic insecticidal protein. This toxin does not kill the Bacillus (bacterium) because it exists as inactive protoxins in them. But, once an insect ingests it, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the alimentary canal. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause swelling and lysis and finally cause the death of the insect.

questions.text: The crops engineered for glyphosate are resistant/tolerant to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glyphosate-tolerant crops are not engineered for insect resistance. Their primary genetic modification is related to tolerance to specific herbicides, not resistance to insects.
Glyphosate is a broad-spectrum herbicide and therefore, crops engineered for glyphosate are resistant/tolerant to herbicides.
Glyphosate-tolerant crops are not engineered to be resistant to fungi. Their primary modification is related to herbicide tolerance, not resistance to fungal pathogens.
Glyphosate-tolerant crops are not engineered to be resistant to bacteria. The primary modification is related to herbicide tolerance, not bacterial resistance.

questions.explanation: Glyphosate is a broad-spectrum herbicide that especially kills broad-leaved herbs. Crop plants may also get affected by the herbicide, thus now crop plants are genetically engineered for glyphosate resistance. So, when glyphosate herbicide is applied, only weeds and no crop plants get harmed.

questions.text: The first human hormone that was produced by recombinant DNA technology was:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The first human hormone produced by recombinant DNA technology is insulin
Estrogen is a hormone produced in the human body naturally.
Thyroxin is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland in the human body.
Corpus luteum is a gland that produces progesterone.

questions.explanation: The recombinant DNA technological processes have made a great impact in the area of health care by mass production of safe and more effective therapeutic drugs. In 1983, Eli Lily, an American company, first prepared two DNA sequences corresponding to A and B chains of human insulin and introduced them in plasmids of Escherichia coli to produce insulin chains. Chains A and B were produced separately, extracted, and combined by creating disulfide bonds to form human insulin (humulin).

questions.text: RNA interference involves the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Synthesis of complementary DNA and RNA using reverse transcriptase
options.explanation: cDNA synthesis is a process of producing complementary DNA (cDNA) by reverse transcription. The process utilizes mRNA or miRNA as a template, reverse transcriptase enzyme, and a thermostable primer, complementary to the 3' end of the RNA template.

options.text: Silencing of specific mRNA due to complementary RNA
options.explanation: RNA interference involves the silencing of a specific mRNA due to complementary dsRNA molecules.

options.text: Interference of RNA in the synthesis of DNA
options.explanation: RNA interference (RNAi) is a phenomenon in which small double-stranded RNA molecules induce sequence-specific degradation of homologous single-stranded RNA (1). In plants and insects, RNAi activity plays a role in host-cell protection from viruses and transposons (2, 3).

The process of making mRNA from DNA is called transcription, and it occurs in the nucleus. The mRNA directs the synthesis of proteins, which occurs in the cytoplasm. mRNA formed in the nucleus is transported out of the nucleus and into the cytoplasm where it attaches to the ribosomes.

questions.explanation: RNA interference (RNAi) is the phenomenon of inhibiting the activity of a gene through the production of both sense and antisense RNA. RNAi takes place in all eukaryotic organisms as a method of cellular defense. This method involves specific mRNA silencing. It is due to a complementary dsRNA molecule that binds to and prevents translation of the mRNA causing its silencing.

questions.text: Which one of the following vectors is used to replace the defective gene in gene therapy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenovirus vectors can be replication-defective; certain essential viral genes are deleted and replaced by a cassette that expresses a foreign therapeutic gene. Such vectors are used for gene therapy, as vaccines, and for cancer therapy.

Cosmids are a type of hybrid plasmid containing a lambda phage cos sequence, widely used in cloning vectors in genetic engineering. Cosmid is different from Phagemid in the type of sequence contained. Cosmids comprise a cos site and a plasmid, which is a hybrid vector.

Ri is the root-inducing plasmid seen in Agrobacterium rhizogenes. They induce hairy root diseases in dicots. Its virulence plasmid is called pRi (Ri-Plasmid). The T-DNA of the Ri plasmid infects the plants by entering through their wounds. They integrate and express their bacterial genome within the plant.

The Ti-plasmid in the bacteria is known to induce crown gall disease in plants by transferring crucial regions from the plasmid. These crucial regions were seen to modify the plant cells into a tumor to produce synthetic plant hormones and cause crown gall.

questions.explanation: Gene therapy is a corrective therapy that is given to patients with diseases caused by some gene defects. Here, genes are inserted into a person’s cells and tissues to treat the disease by replacing the defective gene. The normal gene delivered into the individual or embryo takes over the function and compensates for the normal gene. Viral vectors like adenovirus are generally used to deliver the normal gene.

questions.text: Consumption of which one of the following foods can prevent the kind of blindness associated with vitamin ‘A’ deficiency?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Flavr Savr was designed to be able to be left longer to ripen on the vine (many natural tomatoes are picked unripe and then ripened through chemical means) and to have a longer shelf life than natural tomatoes.

It is Generally Recognized as safe (GRAS) for human consumption, including use in infant formula. Canola oil is eligible to carry a qualified health claim from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for its ability to reduce the risk of coronary heart disease when used in place of saturated fat in the diet.
The advantage of golden rice is that it can be used in areas where Vitamin A deficiency is common and so can help prevent blindness.

Bt eggplant specifically targets the fruit and shoot borer and related pests such as the fruit borer and stem borer. As such it is ineffective against other insects. This helps ensure that beneficial insects and organisms continue to thrive and are not affected as usually occurs in the case of pesticide sprays.

questions.explanation: Golden rice is a transgenic variety of rice (Oryza sativa) that contains good quantities of B-carotene (provitamin A-inactive state of vitamin A). B-carotene is a principal source of vitamin A. The grains of this rice are yellow in color due to B­-carotene and are commonly called golden rice.

questions.text: Tobacco plants resistant to a nematode have been developed by the introduction of DNA that produces (in the host cells):
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tobacco plant resistance to a nematode has been developed by the introduction of DNA that produces both sense and antisense RNA.
Tobacco plants resistant to nematodes are not typically developed by introducing DNA that produces a specific hormone. The resistance is often achieved through other mechanisms.

An antifeedant is a substance that deters feeding by herbivores but is not a common approach for developing nematode-resistant tobacco plants. The primary strategy is introducing toxic proteins that harm the nematodes rather than deterring their feeding.
Nematode-resistant tobacco plants are often developed by introducing DNA that produces a toxic protein. This protein can be harmful to nematodes, effectively protecting the plant from their damage.

questions.explanation: Many nematodes live in plants and animals including human beings. A nematode Meloidogyne incognita infests the roots of tobacco plants and causes a great reduction in yield. A novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infection that was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi). RNA interference (RNAi) is the phenomenon of inhibiting the activity of a gene by the synthesis of RNA molecules complementary to the mRNA. The normal (in vivo synthesized) mRNA of a gene is said to be “sense” because it carries the codons that are “read” during translation. Normally, the complement to the mRNA “sense” strand will not contain a sequence of codons that can be translated to produce a functional protein; thus, this complementary strand is called “anti­-sense RNA”. The anti-­sense RNA and mRNA molecules will anneal to form duplex RNA molecules (or double-stranded RNA) and the duplex RNA molecules can not be translated. Thus, the presence of anti­sense RNA will block translation of the mRNA of the affected gene. In fact, recent evidence indicates that these RNA duplexes are often rapidly degraded in vivo.

questions.text: What forms the basis of DNA fingerprinting?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The relative proportions of purines and pyrimidines in DNA
options.explanation: DNA fingerprinting is not based on the relative proportions of purines (adenine and guanine) and pyrimidines (cytosine and thymine) in DNA. It focuses on specific DNA sequences.

options.text: The relative difference in the DNA occurrence in blood, skin, and saliva
options.explanation: While DNA can be obtained from blood, skin, and saliva for DNA fingerprinting, this option does not directly address the basis of DNA fingerprinting, which involves analyzing specific DNA sequences, not the source of the DNA.

options.text: The relative amount of DNA in the ridges and grooves of the fingerprints
options.explanation: This option is not related to the basis of DNA fingerprinting. It seems to refer to the physical impressions of fingerprints rather than the genetic analysis of DNA.

options.text: Satellite DNA occurring as highly repeated, short DNA segments
options.explanation: The basis of DNA fingerprinting is VNTR. VNTR is a satellite DNA as a probe showing a high degree of polymorphism. This implies that there are repetitive sequences of DNA that are found across individuals of all species. Scientists identify variations in these sequences.

questions.explanation: DNA fingerprinting is a technique of determining nucleotide sequences of certain areas of DNA which are unique to each individual. The difference of about 0.1% or 3 × 106 base pairs (out of 3 × 10 9 bp) provides individuality to each human being. The human genome possesses numerous small noncoding but inheritable sequences of bases that are repeated many times. These sequences occur near telomere, centromeres, Y chromosome, and heterochromatic area. The area with the same sequence of bases repeated several times is called repetitive DNA. It is separated as a satellite from the bulk DNA during density gradient centrifugation and hence called satellite DNA where repetition of the bases is in tandem. Satellite DNAs show polymorphism (the occurrence of mutations in a population at high frequency), which is the basis of genetic mapping of human genome as well as DNA fingerprinting. While mutations in genes produce alleles with different expressions, mutations in noncoding repetitive DNA have no immediate impact. These mutations which have piled up with time form the basis of polymorphism.

questions.text: The first clinical gene therapy was given for treating:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gene therapy for diabetes mellitus had been a subject of research, but there was no established gene therapy treatment widely available for diabetes. The treatment of diabetes primarily relied on other approaches such as insulin therapy, oral medications, and lifestyle management.

Gene therapy for chickenpox is not a standard or established treatment. Chickenpox is caused by the varicella-zoster virus, and traditionally, the primary means of managing chickenpox and related conditions like shingles has been through vaccination and antiviral medications. Gene therapy has not been a common or established approach for managing chickenpox or related viral infections.

RA is a complex autoimmune disease, and its treatment primarily involves medications, physical therapy, and other non-genetic therapeutic approaches. Gene therapy research was exploring the potential for targeted interventions in RA, but no specific gene therapy had been approved or widely used for the treatment of RA.

The first successful clinical trials for gene therapy to treat Adenosine Deaminase (ADA) deficiency, a form of Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID), were conducted in the late 1980s and early 1990s. These trials marked a significant milestone in the development of gene therapy.

questions.explanation: Gene therapy is a collection of methods that allows the correction of a gene defect that has been diagnosed in a child/embryo. Here genes are inserted into a person’s cells and tissues to treat a disease. Correction of a genetic defect involves the delivery of a normal gene into the individual or embryo to take over the function of and compensate for the non­functional gene. The first clinical gene therapy was given in 1990 to a 4- year old girl with adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency. This enzyme is very important for the immune system to function. SCID is caused due to defect in the gene for the enzyme adenosine deaminase. In some children, ADA deficiency can be cured by bone marrow transplantation. Here, the isolated gene from bone marrow cells producing ADA is introduced into cells at early embryonic stages; it can be a permanent cure.

questions.text: The process of RNA interference (RNAi) has been used in the development of plants resistant to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The process of RNA interference has been used in the development of plants resistant to nematodes.

Fungi are generally decomposers, meaning they get their nourishment from decomposing or dead organic materials (usually plants). Insoluble polysaccharides, such as cellulose and lignin in dead wood, can be broken down by fungal exoenzymes into readily absorbable glucose molecules.

A virus is an infectious microorganism made up of a nucleic acid segment (DNA or RNA) encased in a protein coat. A virus cannot replicate on its own; instead, it must infect cells and use host cell components to replicate itself.

Any little creature has six legs and three segments to its body Insects frequently have wings as well. Insects include ants, bees, and flies. Any member of the largest class of the phylum Arthropoda, which is itself the largest of the animal phyla, is an insect (class Insecta or Hexapoda).

questions.explanation: Many nematodes live in plants and animals including human beings. A nematode Meloidogyne incognita infests the roots of tobacco plants and causes a great reduction in yield. A novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infection that was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi). RNA interference (RNAi) is the phenomenon of inhibiting the activity of a gene by the synthesis of RNA molecules complementary to the mRNA. The normal (in vivo synthesized) mRNA of a gene is said to be “sense” because it carries the codons that are “read” during translation. Normally, the complement to the mRNA “sense” strand will not contain a sequence of codons that can be translated to produce a functional protein; thus, this complementary strand is called “anti-­sense RNA”. The anti­-sense RNA and mRNA molecules will anneal to form duplex RNA molecules (or double-stranded RNA) and the duplex RNA molecules can not be translated. Thus, the presence of anti­sense RNA will block translation of the mRNA of the affected gene.

questions.text: Bacillus thuringiensis forms protein crystals that contain insecticidal protein. This protein:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Binds with epithelial cells in the midgut of the insect pest ultimately killing it
options.explanation: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) produces insecticidal proteins that, when ingested by insect pests, bind to receptors on the midgut epithelial cells, causing damage to the gut lining and ultimately leading to the death of the insect. This is a well-known mechanism of action for Bt proteins, which are widely used in genetically modified crops and insecticides to control insect pests.

options.text: Is coded by several genes including the gene cry
options.explanation: Bt proteins are encoded by specific genes, but it is not typically multiple genes. The proteins are produced by the bacterium and then released.

options.text: Is activated by acid pH of the foregut of the insect pest
options.explanation: Bt proteins do not require an acidic pH to be activated; they are effective once ingested by the insect pest and exposed to the alkaline conditions of the insect's gut.

options.text: Does not kill the carrier bacterium which is itself resistant to this toxin
options.explanation: Bt proteins do not kill the carrier bacterium, as Bt itself is naturally resistant to the toxin it produces.

questions.explanation: Soil bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis produces proteins that kill certain insects like lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles), and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms some protein crystals which contain a toxic insecticidal protein. This toxin does not kill the Bacillus (bacterium) because it exists as inactive protoxins in them. But, once an insect ingests it, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the alimentary canal. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause swelling and lysis and finally cause the death of the insect.

questions.text: Read the following four statements. (A) The first transgenic buffalo, Rosie, produced milk that was human alpha­ lactalbumin enriched. (B) Restriction enzymes are used in the isolation of DNA from other macromolecules. (C) Downstream processing is one of the steps of rDNA technology. (D) Disarmed pathogen vectors are also used in the transfer of rDNA into the host. Which of the two statements have mistakes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: B. Restriction enzymes are the enzymes produced by certain bacteria that have the property of cleaving DNA molecules at or near specific base sequences. So, it is used in the isolation of a part of DNA from the whole DNA macromolecule. It is not used to isolate DNA from other macromolecules. Enzymes like proteinase K are used to separate DNA from other macromolecules like histone proteins. So, the statement that restriction enzymes are used in the isolation of DNA from other macromolecules is wrong. C. Downstream processing refers to the series of unit operations used to isolate, purify, and concentrate the product obtained by r DNA. Downstream processing includes the separation and purification process. Thus, downstream processing is one of the steps of rDNA technology.

C. Downstream processing refers to the series of unit operations used to isolate, purify, and concentrate the product obtained by r DNA. Downstream processing includes the separation and purification process. Thus, downstream processing is one of the steps of rDNA technology. D. Disarmed pathogens are created by removing the genes that are responsible for virulence and adding genes of our interest instead so that when the disarmed pathogen is used to infect the host, it does not cause disease but transfers the recombinant DNA into the host.

A. The first transgenic cow was Rosie, developed in 1997, in which the gene introduced was Human-alpha-lactalbumin milk at 2.4 grams per liter. As a result, the milk produced had a better content of protein and was nutritionally more apt for human infants. This was because the milk was a nutritionally more balanced product compared to the natural bovine milk and could be served to the elderly or babies having special digestive or nutritive requirements. Hence, the statement given in the question that Rosie was the first transgenic buffalo is wrong. Rosie was a cow. C. Downstream processing refers to the series of unit operations used to isolate, purify, and concentrate the product obtained by r DNA. Downstream processing includes the separation and purification process. Thus, downstream processing is one of the steps of rDNA technology.

A. The first transgenic cow was Rosie, developed in 1997, in which the gene introduced was Human-alpha-lactalbumin milk at 2.4 grams per litre. As a result, the milk produced had a better content of protein and was nutritionally more apt for human infants. This was because the milk was a nutritionally more balanced product compared to the natural bovine milk and could be served to the elderly or babies having special digestive or nutritive requirements. Hence, the statement given in the question that Rosie was the first transgenic buffalo is wrong.Rosie was a cow. B. Restriction enzymes are the enzymes produced by certain bacteria that have the property of cleaving DNA molecules at or near specific base sequences. So, it is used in the isolation of a part of DNA from the whole DNA macromolecule. It is not used to isolate DNA from other macromolecules. Enzymes like proteinase K are used to separate out DNA from the other macromolecules like histone proteins. So, the statement that restriction enzymes are used in the isolation of DNA from other macromolecules is wrong.

questions.explanation: In 1997, the first transgenic cow, Rosie, produced human protein enriched milk. The milk contained the human alpha ­lactalbumin and was nutritionally a more balanced product for human babies than natural cow­milk. Isolation of DNA from other macromolecule is achieved by treating the bacterial cells/plant or animal tissue with enzymes such as lysozyme (bacteria), cellulase (plant cells), chitinase (fungus).

questions.text: Genetic engineering has been successfully used to produce:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Transgenic mice for testing the safety of polio vaccine before use in humans
options.explanation: Genetic engineering has been used to create transgenic mice that can serve as models for studying the safety and effectiveness of vaccines, including the polio vaccine before they are tested in humans. These mice are genetically modified to mimic human responses to the vaccine and help researchers assess its safety and efficacy.

options.text: Transgenic models for studying new treatments for certain cardiac diseases
options.explanation: Genetic engineering has been used to develop transgenic animal models, such as mice, that carry specific genetic mutations associated with cardiac diseases. These models are valuable for studying the underlying mechanisms of the diseases and for testing potential treatments.

options.text: Transgenic cow­-Rosie which produces high fat milk for making ghee
options.explanation: Genetic engineering has been used to create transgenic cows like Rosie, which produce milk with specific traits, such as higher fat content. This milk can be used to make products like ghee, a clarified butter widely used in cooking.

options.text: Animals like bulls for farm work as they have extra power
options.explanation: Genetic engineering can be used to enhance specific traits in animals for practical purposes, such as disease resistance, productivity, or nutritional content, but it does not confer superpowers or abilities beyond their natural biology. Such claims are typically fictional or speculative and not based on scientific reality.

questions.explanation: Many transgenic animals are designed to increase our understanding of how genes contribute to the development of diseases. These are specially made to serve as models for human diseases so that investigation of new treatments for diseases is made possible. Today transgenic models exist for many human diseases such as cancer, cystic fibrosis, rheumatoid arthritis and Alzheimer’s. Transgenic mice are being developed for use in testing the safety of vaccines before they are used on humans. Transgenic mice are being used to test the safety of the polio vaccine.

questions.text: Some of the characteristics of Bt cotton are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Long fibre and resistance to aphids
options.explanation: This option suggests that Bt cotton has both long fibers (referring to cotton fiber length) and resistance to aphids. While Bt cotton is known for its insect resistance, it primarily targets specific pests like bollworms, not aphids. Cotton fiber length can vary among different cotton varieties, but this option does not emphasize the most prominent characteristic of Bt cotton.

options.text: Medium yield, long fibre and resistance to beetle pests
options.explanation: This option combines multiple characteristics, including medium yield, long fibers, and resistance to beetle pests. While Bt cotton does provide resistance to specific insect pests, it's known for high yield and insect resistance against dipteran pests (not beetles). The medium yield mentioned here is not a primary feature of Bt cotton.

options.text: High yield and production of toxic protein crystals which kill dipteran pests
options.explanation: Bt cotton is known for its high yield, and the key feature of Bt cotton is its production of toxic protein crystals from Bacillus thuringiensis, which effectively kills dipteran pests like certain types of moths and flies.

options.text: High yield and resistance to bollworms.
options.explanation: High yield is attributed to the resistance against pests, with an emphasis on bollworm resistance. Bollworm resistance is one of the primary features of Bt cotton, and high yield is an important outcome.

questions.explanation: Bt toxin genes were isolated from Bacillus thuringiensis and incorporated into the cotton plant. The genetically modified crop is called Bt cotton. Bt cotton has the following useful characteristics: pest resistance, herbicide tolerance, high-yield, and resistance to bollworm infestation.

questions.text: Which one of the following is now being commercially produced by biotechnological procedures?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nicotine is a natural stimulant that transfers messages quicker than normal between the brain and body. It is a plant-based drug belonging to the Solanaceae family.
Morphine is a medication against pain. It is obtained from the Opioid plant family. And it can be produced easily through plant material as per its demand.

Quinine is obtained from Cinchona trees as it is a natural Cinchona alkaloid. Quinine helps to cure malaria, muscle cramps, and hypersensitivity. As it is plant-based, and available commonly in America and Africa, it can be produced easily by the same method.

Insulin is a hormone necessary for maintaining normal blood glucose levels. In a major disorder like diabetes, insulin plays an important role in the health of the patient. Earlier insulin was obtained from pigs and other animals to treat patients but the method was improper, time-consuming, and can cause infection in patients. Since the discovery of rDNA technology, insulin has been produced with the help of E. coli bacteria. This method is more prominent and can help with large-scale production easily.

questions.explanation: Insulin is now being commercially produced by genetic engineering. Insulin consists of two short polypeptide chains: chain A and chain B, that are linked together by disulfide bonds. Insulin, in mammals, is synthesized as a prohormone which contains an extra stretch called the C­-peptide.In 1988 an American company prepared functional insulin from two DNA sequences corresponding to A and B chains of human insulin and introduced them in plasmids of E.coli to produce insulin chains. In this way, chains A and B were produced separately which was extracted, combined by creating disulfide bonds to get human insulin.

questions.text: What is true about the Bt toxin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Bt protein exists as active toxin in the Bacillus
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Bt toxin exists in an inactive form within the Bacillus thuringiensis bacterium. It is only activated when it encounters the alkaline conditions of the insect gut.

options.text: The activated toxin enters the ovaries of the pest to sterilise it and thus prevent its multiplication
options.explanation: This statement is not generally accurate. Bt toxin primarily acts in the gut of the pest, where it causes damage and leads to the death of the insect. It does not typically enter the ovaries or act as a sterilizing agent.

This statement is not typically accurate. The Bacillus thuringiensis bacterium produces Bt toxins, but it does not typically produce antitoxins. The primary role of Bt toxins is to target specific insect pests, and they are not typically counteracted by antitoxins produced by the bacterium itself.

options.text: The inactive protoxin gets converted into active form in the insect gut
options.explanation: This statement is true. Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) toxin exists in an inactive form in the bacterial cells. When ingested by insect larvae, the alkaline conditions of the insect gut activate the toxin, converting it into its active form. The active toxin then exerts its toxic effects on the insect's gut, ultimately leading to its death.

questions.explanation: Soil bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis produces proteins that kill certain insects like lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles), and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms some protein crystals which contain a toxic insecticidal protein. This toxin does not kill the Bacillus (bacterium) because it exists as inactive protoxins in them. But, once an insect ingests it, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the alimentary canal. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause swelling and lysis and finally cause the death of the insect.

questions.text: Transgenic plants are the ones:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Generated by introducing foreign DNA into a cell and regenerating a plant from that cell
options.explanation: This option correctly describes how transgenic plants are created. Transgenic plants are produced by introducing foreign DNA (transgenes) into plant cells through techniques like Agrobacterium-mediated transformation or biolistic particle bombardment. The transformed cells are then used to regenerate whole plants with the desired traits.

options.text: Produced after protoplast fusion in artificial medium
options.explanation: Protoplast fusion is a technique used in plant breeding, but it does not specifically create transgenic plants. It involves the fusion of plant cells with their cell walls removed. Transgenic plants are typically created through the introduction of foreign DNA into plant cells and the regeneration of whole plants from those transformed cells.

options.text: Grown in artificial medium after hybridization in the field
options.explanation: This option does not accurately describe transgenic plants. Transgenic plants are created by introducing foreign DNA into plant cells and regenerating a plant from those modified cells. This process typically takes place in a laboratory or controlled environment, not in the field.

options.text: Produced by a somatic embryo in artificial medium
options.explanation: This option is not an accurate description of transgenic plants. Somatic embryogenesis is a tissue culture technique used for clonal propagation of plants, but it is not the primary method for producing transgenic plants. Transgenic plants are generated by introducing foreign DNA into plant cells and then regenerating whole plants from those transformed cells.

questions.explanation: The plants produced through genetic engineering contain genes, usually from an unrelated organism. Such genes are called transgenes and the plants having transgenes are called transgenic plants. Recombinant DNA techniques are being used to improve crop plants by increasing their productivity, making them more nutritious, and by developing disease resistance. Transgenic plants have a natural resistance to herbicides and pests. In the future, plants may have an ability to fix atmospheric nitrogen and an increased ability to grow in arid and salty soils.

questions.text: The bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis is widely used in contemporary biology as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is widely used as a natural insecticide. It produces insecticidal proteins that are toxic to certain insect pests. Bt-based insecticides are used in agriculture to protect crops from insect damage and are considered environmentally friendly alternatives to chemical pesticides.

options.text: Agent for production of dairy products
options.explanation: This is not the primary or typical use of Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt). It is not used as an agent for producing dairy products. It is primarily known for its application as an insecticide.

While some Bacillus species are used as sources of industrial enzymes, Bacillus thuringiensis is more widely known for its production of insecticidal proteins. It is not commonly used as a source of industrial enzymes.

Bacillus thuringiensis is not typically used as an indicator of water pollution. Indicator organisms for water quality assessment are usually selected for their sensitivity to pollutants, and Bt is not used for this purpose.

questions.explanation: Soil bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis produces proteins that kill certain insects like lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles), and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). B. thuringiensis forms some protein crystals which contain a toxic insecticidal protein. This toxin does not kill the Bacillus (bacterium) because it exists as inactive protoxins in them. But, once an insect ingests it, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the alimentary canal. The activated toxin binds to the surface of midgut epithelial cells and creates pores that cause swelling and lysis and finally cause the death of the insect.

questions.text: The main objective of the production/use of herbicide-resistant GM crops is to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ​Ecofriendly herbicides are less harmful to the plants and also to those who ingest them. GM crops are not required to counteract that.

options.text: Reduce herbicide accumulation in food articles for health safety
options.explanation: Herbicides are used to grow crops devoid of any possible herbs that compete with the main crop in the agricultural land.The accumulation of herbicides can have detrimental effects on those who consume the food. The ​production of herbicide-resistant GM crops is done to reduce the accumulation of herbicides so that individuals can get the nutrients from food.

options.text: Eliminate weeds from the field without the use of manual labour
options.explanation: Eliminating weeds is done by the weedicides and hence it cannot be performed by the GM crops.

options.text: Eliminate weeds from the field without the use of herbicides.
options.explanation: ​The weeds are removed with the help of herbicides that farmers spread on the field.

questions.explanation: Genetic engineering has helped to develop such transgenic crop plants which are resistant to herbicides so that they are not damaged when farmers spray herbicides in the fields. Herbicide resistant plants have been developed in such a way that they continue to produce normal crop yield and at the same time remain unaffected by the activity of herbicides. These plants also reduces the use of weeding labour, farmer’s cost and increases yield.

questions.text: Human insulin is being commercially produced from a transgenic species of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rhizobium is used for nitrogen fixation in the root nodules of legume plants.
Saccharomyces is commonly used for brewing and baking (yeast).

Coli, or Escherichia coli, is a commonly used host organism in biotechnology for the production of human insulin and various other recombinant proteins. To produce human insulin through genetic engineering, the human insulin gene is inserted into the E. coli bacterium, which acts as a host to produce the insulin protein. E. coli is often chosen as a host organism because it is easy to manipulate genetically, grows rapidly, and has well-understood genetics and growth characteristics. It has been used extensively in the pharmaceutical and biotechnology industries for the large-scale production of biopharmaceuticals like insulin.
Mycobacterium is associated with diseases like tuberculosis and is not typically used for the production of human insulin.

questions.explanation: Insulin is now being commercially produced by genetic engineering. Insulin consists of two short polypeptide chains: chain A and chain B, that are linked together by disulphide bonds. Insulin in mammals is synthesized as a prohormone which contains an extra stretch called the C-­peptide. During maturation, this C­-peptide is removed. The production of insulin could only have been commercially possible if somehow the maturation process of C­-peptide could've been skipped.This problem was solved in 1988 by Eli Lilly, an American company that prepared functional insulin from two DNA sequences corresponding to A and B chains of human insulin and introduced them in plasmids of E.coli to produce insulin chains. In this way, chains A and B were produced separately, extracted and combined by creating disulfide bonds to get human insulin.

questions.text: A genetically engineered micro­organism used successfully in bioremediation of oil spills is a species of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trichoderma is a genus of fungi used in agriculture and plant disease control.
Xanthomonas is a genus of bacteria known for causing plant diseases.
Bacillus is a genus of bacteria used in various industrial and agricultural applications but not primarily for oil spill cleanup.

Pseudomonas is a genus of bacteria, and some species within this genus have been genetically engineered for bioremediation purposes, including the degradation of oil spills. These bacteria can be modified to enhance their ability to break down hydrocarbons found in oil, making them effective for cleaning up oil-contaminated environments. Pseudomonas putida, for example, has been studied and engineered for its oil-degrading capabilities.

questions.explanation: Bioremediation is the process of using living micro­organisms to clean up a contaminated site. Micro­organisms do this by removing toxins from materials. They decompose these compounds by using enzymes, specific proteins that control reactions in living cells. Organisms that produce enzymes capable of degrading petroleum are useful in cleaning up oil spills. Some common ones that break down oil are genetically engineered species of Pseudomonas and Azotobacter. Bioremediation accounts for 5 to 10 percent of all pollution treatment and has been used successfully in cleaning up leaking underground gasoline storage tanks. Bioremediation has many applications, from ordinary garden compost to the removal of selenium and other toxic metals from waste. The best agents for bioremediation are the ones that can break down contaminants without becoming contaminated or harmful themselves.

questions.text: Golden rice is a promising transgenic crop. When released for cultivation, it will help in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Producing a petrol­like fuel from rice
options.explanation: This option is not a primary purpose for transgenic crop development. It pertains to biofuel production, which typically involves converting plant materials like rice into bioethanol or biodiesel, but it is separate from transgenic crop development for food or feed purposes.

Golden rice is a genetically modified variety of rice that has been engineered to produce beta-carotene, a precursor of vitamin A. Vitamin A deficiency is a significant health issue in many parts of the world, particularly in developing countries where rice is a dietary staple. The incorporation of beta-carotene in golden rice can help address this deficiency, as it allows people to obtain a dietary source of vitamin A from rice consumption. This has the potential to improve the health and well-being of populations that rely heavily on rice as a primary food source.

Pest-resistant transgenic crops are genetically modified to be less susceptible to pests. They can produce toxins harmful to pests or bolster their defenses, reducing the need for chemical pesticides and protecting crops from pest damage. This can increase crop yields and reduce the environmental impact of agriculture.

Transgenic crops with herbicide tolerance are genetically modified to withstand specific herbicides. This allows farmers to use these herbicides to control weeds without harming the crop, simplifying weed management and reducing the need for manual labor.

questions.explanation: Golden rice is a transgenic variety of rice (Oryza sativa) which contains good quantities of B­-carotene (provitamin A-inactive state of vitamin A). B­-carotene is a principal source of vitamin A. Since the grains of this rice is yellow in colour due to B­-carotene and commonly called golden rice.

questions.text: Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) strains have been used for designing novel:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Biofertilizers are microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, or other living organisms) that are used to improve soil fertility and enhance plant growth. They work by fixing atmospheric nitrogen, solubilizing phosphate, or promoting nutrient availability in the soil. Biofertilizers are not directly related to Bt strains, which are used for insecticidal purposes.

Bio-metallurgical techniques involve the use of microorganisms in mining and metallurgy. Certain bacteria and fungi can aid in the extraction of metals from ores or help in metal recovery processes. This application is unrelated to the use of Bt strains for insecticidal purposes.

Bio-mineralization is the natural or induced process of mineral formation facilitated by microorganisms. Some microorganisms are involved in mineral precipitation or dissolution, affecting the formation and alteration of minerals in geological and environmental processes. This process is distinct from the use of Bt strains for insecticidal purposes.

Bio-insecticidal plants are genetically modified crops that produce proteins toxic to specific insect pests. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) strains have been used to create such plants, making them resistant to certain pests and reducing the need for chemical pesticides. This technology directly involves the use of Bt strains to design crops for insect control.

questions.explanation: Bacillus thuringiensis strains have been used for designing bioinsecticidal plants. A gene from this bacteria has insecticidal property which is transferred to cotton plants to produce Bt cotton which is resistant to bollworm insect which is a major pest of cotton. Similarly, insects affecting maize; cabbage, sunflower etc., are also controlled by mutant strains of Bacillus thuringiensis bacteria.

questions.text: Production of a human protein in bacteria by genetic engineering is possible because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The human chromosome can replicate in bacterial cell
options.explanation: Human chromosomes cannot replicate in bacterial cells. Bacterial and human DNA replication processes are different, and the machinery used for replication is not interchangeable between the two. This statement is not accurate.

options.text: The mechanism of gene regulation is identical in humans and bacteria
options.explanation: The gene regulation mechanisms in humans and bacteria are different. Bacterial gene regulation is typically simpler and involves operons, while eukaryotic gene regulation is more complex with transcription factors, enhancers, and promoters. Therefore, this statement is not correct.

options.text: Bacterial cell can carry out the RNA splicing reactions
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Bacterial cells typically do not perform RNA splicing reactions. RNA splicing, which involves the removal of introns and joining of exons, is a process more commonly associated with eukaryotic cells, such as those in humans.

The genetic code is indeed universal. This means that the same set of codons (triplets of nucleotides) corresponds to the same amino acids in almost all living organisms, including bacteria and humans. This universality allows for the expression of human genes in bacterial cells through genetic engineering.

questions.explanation: Genetic code may be defined as the sequence of nucleotides in polynucleotide chain which determines the sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain. Thus the genetic code is universal. There is no ambiguity regarding the genetic code. It means that each codon codes for the same amino acid in all organisms including bacteria, plants and animals.

questions.text: Golden rice is a transgenic crop of the future with the following improved traits:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golden rice is primarily designed to address vitamin A deficiency and is not engineered for insect resistance. Insect-resistant crops, like Bt cotton and Bt corn, are designed to protect against specific insect pests by producing insecticidal proteins, but this is not the primary goal of golden rice.

Golden rice is not specifically engineered for high lysine content. Lysine is an essential amino acid, and while it's important for nutrition, the main focus of golden rice is on increasing vitamin A levels.

Golden rice is not primarily engineered for high protein content. It was developed to address vitamin A deficiency by increasing the levels of beta-carotene (a precursor of vitamin A) in the rice grains.

Golden rice is genetically modified to have high levels of beta-carotene, a precursor of vitamin A. This makes it a valuable tool for combating vitamin A deficiency, which is a significant health issue in some parts of the world.

questions.explanation: Golden rice is a transgenic crop rice with high vitamin A content. It has been developed by transferring beta carotene synthesizing gene into the transgenic rice. Beta carotene is the precursor of vitamin A. This transgenic rice has been crossed with the already adapted varieties of rice to make them grow well in a particular area. It is very useful for people suffering from vision impairment due to vitamin A deficiency.

questions.text: DNA fingerprinting refers to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Molecular analysis of profiles of DNA samples
options.explanation: DNA fingerprinting refers to the molecular analysis of unique DNA profiles from different individuals. It involves analyzing specific regions of an individual's DNA to create a distinctive pattern that can be used for identification, particularly in forensic science and paternity testing.

options.text: Analysis of DNA samples using imprinting devices
options.explanation: DNA fingerprinting does not involve the use of an "imprinting device." It is a laboratory technique used to analyze and compare DNA samples for identification and other purposes.

options.text: Techniques used for molecular analysis of different specimens of DNA
options.explanation: While this option captures the essence of DNA fingerprinting, it is a bit broad. DNA fingerprinting is a specific set of techniques used to analyze and compare unique DNA profiles from different individuals or specimens, typically for identification and forensic purposes.

options.text: Techniques used for identification of fingerprints of individuals
options.explanation: This option refers to traditional fingerprint analysis, which is distinct from DNA fingerprinting. Traditional fingerprint analysis involves the examination of physical fingerprints left on surfaces, while DNA fingerprinting is focused on analyzing genetic material.

questions.explanation: DNA fingerprinting is a technique of determining nucleotide sequences of certain areas of DNA which are unique to each individual. The difference of about 0.1% or 3 × 10 6 base pairs (out of 3 × 10 9 bp) provides individuality to each human being. The human genome possesses numerous small noncoding but inheritable sequences of bases that are repeated many times. These sequences occur near telomere, centromeres, Y chromosome and heterochromatic area. The area with the same sequence of bases repeated several times is called repetitive DNA. It is separated as satellite from the bulk DNA during density gradient centrifugation and hence called satellite DNA where repetition of bases is in tandem. Satellite DNAs show polymorphism (the occurrence of mutations in a population at high frequency), which is the basis of genetic mapping of the human genome as well as DNA fingerprinting. While mutations in genes produce alleles with different expressions, mutations in noncoding repetitive DNA have no immediate impact. These mutations which have piled up with time form the basis of polymorphism.

questions.text: The Ti plasmid is often used for making transgenic plants. The plasmid is found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Ti (tumor-inducing) plasmid is not typically found in Azotobacter. Azotobacter is a genus of free-living, nitrogen-fixing bacteria, and it is not known for harboring the Ti plasmid.

options.text: Rhizobium of the roots of leguminous plants
options.explanation: The Ti plasmid is not typically found in Rhizobium either. Rhizobium is known for forming symbiotic relationships with the roots of leguminous plants but does not contain the Ti plasmid.

The Ti (tumor-inducing) plasmid is commonly found in Agrobacterium. Agrobacterium tumefaciens, a species of Agrobacterium, is used in genetic engineering to transfer DNA, including genes of interest, into plants. The Ti plasmid is a natural genetic tool of Agrobacterium that facilitates this process.
The Ti plasmid is not found in yeast as a 2 μm plasmid. Yeast may contain various plasmids, but the Ti plasmid is specific to Agrobacterium and is not naturally present in yeast.

questions.explanation: Ti plasmid (tumor inducing) from the soil bacterium Agrobacterium tumefaciens is effectively used as a vector for gene transfer to plant cells. The part of Ti plasmid transferred into plant cell DNA is called the T-­DNA. This T­-DNA with desired DNA spliced into it is inserted into the chromosomes of the host plant where it produces copies of itself, by migrating from one chromosomal position to another at random. Such plant cells are then cultured, induced to multiply and differentiate to form plantlets. Transferred into the soil, the plantlets grow into mature plants, carrying the foreign gene, expressed throughout the new plant.

questions.text: ELISA is used to detect viruses where the key reagent is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase is not typically the key reagent used in ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay) to detect viruses. In ELISA, enzymes like horseradish peroxidase or alkaline phosphatase are used as labels to produce a detectable signal when the target virus or viral components bind to specific antibodies.

Catalase is not the key reagent used in ELISA to detect viruses. Catalase is an enzyme involved in the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide to water and oxygen, and it is not a standard component of ELISA assays.

A DNA probe is not typically used as the key reagent in ELISA for virus detection. ELISA assays are more commonly associated with the use of antibodies (antibody-based ELISA) or antigens (antigen-based ELISA) to detect the presence of viral proteins or particles.

RNAase is not the key reagent used in ELISA to detect viruses. RNase is an enzyme that degrades RNA, and it is not a standard component of ELISA assays. ELISA primarily uses enzymes like horseradish peroxidase or alkaline phosphatase as labels to generate a signal when antibodies or antigens bind to viral components.

questions.explanation: ELISA (enzyme linked immunosorbent assay) screening test is the initial test to diagnose AIDS. The test works by detecting antibodies/ substances or protein which are produced in the blood when the virus is present.Alkaline phosphatase and peroxidases are commonly used enzymes as key reagents to perform the ELISA test. These enzymes are used to provide antibody-­antigen complex in a specialized ELISA plate or tray. In ELISA test for detecting a particular antigen, its antibody is buffered and a drop of serum (supernatant of centrifuged blood) is poured over it. If the latter contains antigen, it will produceantigen­ antibody complex. A second enzyme labelled antibody is added. It forms an enzyme­antigen­ antibody complex if the antigen is present. The substrate is now added. It produces a stain if the antigen is present.

questions.text: The term 'humulin' is used for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Humulin is not a term used for a hydrolytic enzyme. Hydrolytic enzymes are involved in the breakdown of chemical bonds through the addition of water.
Humulin is not an antibiotic. Antibiotics are medications used to treat bacterial infections, while Humulin is used to manage blood sugar levels in diabetes.

The term "Humulin" is used to refer to human insulin, a hormone used to regulate blood sugar levels in individuals with diabetes. It is called "Humulin" to emphasize that it is structurally identical to the insulin produced naturally in the human body.
Humulin is not related to isoenzymes. Isoenzymes are multiple forms of an enzyme that catalyze the same reaction but may have different properties or locations.

questions.explanation: Human insulin (humulin) is the first therapeutic product produced by means of recombinant technology by Eli Lilly and Co. on July 5, 1983.

questions.text: The first transgenic crop was:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tobacco was one of the earliest transgenic plants. In 1983, tobacco plants were the first to be genetically modified with a foreign gene, marking an important milestone in the development of transgenic crops.

Cotton is not typically recognized as the first transgenic crop. While cotton varieties have been genetically modified to express traits such as resistance to insect pests, they were not the first transgenic crops. Tobacco holds that distinction.
Pea is not known to be the first transgenic crop.
Flax is not known to be the first transgenic crop.

questions.explanation: Transgenic plants are those plants in which a foreign gene has been introduced and stably integrated into host DNA. The first transgenic plants were produced in tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum). A gene resistant to PPT (L­-phosphinothricin), an active ingredient of herbicide ‘Basta’, was isolated from Medicago sativa. It inhibits the enzyme GS (glutamine synthase) which is involved in ammonia assimilation. This gene resistant to PPT was incorporated into tobacco, as a result of which transgenic tobacco was produced which was resistant to PPT.

questions.text: The transgenic animals are those which have:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Foreign DNA in all its cells
options.explanation: Transgenic animals may have foreign DNA in all their cells, but it's not a requirement for an animal to be considered transgenic. Often, transgenes are incorporated into specific target cells or tissues rather than all cells of the organism.

options.text: Foreign DNA in some of its cells
options.explanation: Transgenic animals are those that have foreign DNA (transgenes) incorporated into some of their cells. This foreign DNA is usually introduced into specific cells or tissues to confer certain traits or characteristics.

options.text: Foreign DNA and RNA in all its cells
options.explanation: Transgenic animals typically involve foreign DNA but not necessarily foreign RNA in some of their cells. The presence of both foreign DNA and RNA in all cells of an organism would be unusual and is not a common characteristic of transgenic animals.

options.text: Foreign DNA and RNA in some of its cells
options.explanation: Transgenic animals primarily involve the introduction of foreign DNA into some of their cells. The presence of foreign RNA in cells is less common and not a standard characteristic of transgenic animals.

questions.explanation: A transgenic organism is one that has become transformed following the introduction of novel genes into its genome. It is most frequently achieved by the integration of cloned DNA sequences following their injection into the fertilized egg. This fertilized egg divides mitotically to form the whole organism so that all the cells of the organism will carry the transferred gene. The transferred genes are known as transgenes. Transgenesis can be done by pronuclear microinjection and somatic cell nuclear transfer or cloning. Transgenic animals produced by this technology include mice, Drosophila, Xenopus and some of the fish species.

questions.text: The DNA fragments separated on an agarose gel can be visualized after staining with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetocarmine is a stain used in microscopy to colorize cells and make them more visible under a microscope. It is not typically used for visualizing DNA fragments on an agarose gel.
Aniline blue is not commonly used for visualizing DNA fragments on an agarose gel.

Ethidium bromide is the most commonly used dye for visualizing DNA fragments separated on an agarose gel. It binds to DNA and fluoresces under ultraviolet light, allowing for the detection and visualization of DNA bands in the gel.
Bromophenol blue is used as a tracking dye in electrophoresis to monitor the progress of DNA or RNA migration in a gel, but it is not used for visualizing DNA fragments.

questions.explanation: The separated DNA fragments can be seen only after staining them with a compound known as ethidium bromide (EtBr) followed by exposure to UV radiation as bright orange coloured bands.

questions.text: A gene whose expression helps to identify transformed cells is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A vector is a DNA molecule used to carry foreign DNA into a host cell during genetic engineering. It can be a plasmid, phage, or other genetic element. Vectors are essential for delivering foreign DNA but are not used to identify transformed cells.

A plasmid is a small, circular DNA molecule often used as a vector in genetic engineering. It can carry selectable markers and other elements to facilitate the introduction of foreign DNA into a host cell. While plasmids can contain selectable markers, they are not typically used to identify transformed cells; that's the role of the selectable marker contained within the plasmid.
A structural gene is a gene that encodes a protein or functional RNA molecule. It carries out a specific biological function within the cell and is not necessarily used to identify transformed cells.

A selectable marker is a gene introduced into a cell along with the gene of interest during transformation to help identify transformed cells. It typically confers a trait, such as resistance to an antibiotic or the ability to grow in a specific culture medium, making it possible to selectively isolate the transformed cells.

questions.explanation: Some genes called “selectable markers” help in selecting those host cells which contain the vectors (transformants) and eliminating the non­transformants.

questions.text: What criterion is for DNA fragment movement on agarose gel during gel electrophoresis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The smaller the fragment size, the farther it moves
options.explanation: The criterion for DNA fragment movement during gel electrophoresis is that the smaller the DNA fragment, the faster it moves and the farther it migrates through the gel. This is because smaller fragments can pass more easily through the pores of the gel matrix.

options.text: Positively charged fragments move to farther end
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. DNA fragments are negatively charged, and they move towards the positively charged electrode during gel electrophoresis.

options.text: Negatively charged fragments do not move
options.explanation: This statement is not correct. DNA fragments are negatively charged due to the phosphate groups in the DNA backbone, and they do move during gel electrophoresis, migrating toward the positively charged electrode.

options.text: The larger the fragment size, the farther it moves
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. In gel electrophoresis, larger DNA fragments move more slowly through the gel matrix and tend to stay closer to the origin, while smaller fragments move faster and migrate farther from the origin.

questions.explanation: Electrophoresis is a technique used for the separation of substances of different ionic properties. Since the DNA fragments are negatively charged molecules, they can be separated by allowing them to move towards the anode. DNA fragments move towards the anode according to their molecule's size through the pores of agarose gel. Thus, the smaller fragments move farther away as compared to larger fragments.

questions.text: The process of separation and purification of expressed protein before marketing is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The process of separation and purification of expressed proteins after their production, typically for commercial or medical purposes, is known as downstream processing. It involves various steps to isolate and purify the protein product.

Bioprocessing refers to the overall process of using living organisms, such as bacteria or yeast, to produce valuable products, which can include proteins. Downstream processing is a part of the larger bioprocessing workflow.

This term is not commonly used and is less specific. While it might refer to processing steps after a product is manufactured, "downstream processing" is the more appropriate term for the separation and purification of proteins.

Upstream processing involves the steps leading up to protein production, such as cell culture and fermentation. It does not specifically refer to the separation and purification of proteins after production, which is the role of downstream processing.

questions.explanation: After the formation of the product in the bioreactor, it undergoes some processes before a finished product is ready for marketing. The process includes separation and purification of products which is collectively called downstream processing.

questions.text: Stirred-­tank bioreactors have been designed for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stirred-tank bioreactors are not used for the purification of the product. Purification typically occurs in downstream processing after the product is produced in the bioreactor.

options.text: Addition of preservatives to the product
options.explanation: Stirred-tank bioreactors are not primarily designed for the addition of preservatives to the product. They are more commonly used for the cultivation and fermentation of microorganisms for the production of various biotechnological products.

options.text: Availability of oxygen throughout the process
options.explanation: Stirred-tank bioreactors are primarily designed to provide and maintain a continuous supply of oxygen throughout the fermentation process. This is essential for the growth and metabolism of aerobic microorganisms in the bioreactor.

options.text: Ensuring anaerobic conditions in the culture vessel
options.explanation: Stirred-tank bioreactors can be used to control the environment within the culture vessel, but they are more commonly used to ensure aerobic conditions (availability of oxygen) for aerobic microorganisms. For anaerobic conditions, specialized bioreactors are used.

questions.explanation: A stirred­-tank reactor is usually cylindrical or with a curved base to facilitate the mixing of the reactor contents. The stirrer facilitates even mixing and oxygen availability throughout the bioreactor.

questions.text: A foreign DNA and plasmid cut by the same restriction endonuclease can be joined to form a recombinant plasmid using:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eco RI is a restriction endonuclease that is used to cut DNA at specific recognition sites. It is not involved in the joining of DNA fragments but rather in their cleavage.
Taq polymerase is a DNA polymerase often used in the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) to amplify DNA, but it is not used for joining DNA fragments. It lacks ligase activity.
Polymerase II is a DNA polymerase found in eukaryotic cells, and it is not used for joining DNA fragments. Like Taq polymerase, it is not involved in the ligation of DNA fragments.
Ligase is the enzyme used to join (ligate) the foreign DNA and plasmid together. It forms phosphodiester bonds between the DNA fragments, creating a recombinant plasmid.

questions.explanation: Ligase is a class of enzymes that catalyse the formation of covalent bonds using the energy released by the cleavage of ATP. Ligases are important in the synthesis and repair of many biological molecules, including DNA ligase and used in genetic engineering to insert foreign DNA into cloning vectors.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a component of downstream processing?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Separation is a critical component of downstream processing and involves isolating the product from the fermentation broth or culture.
Purification is a key step in downstream processing, focusing on refining and separating the product from contaminants to obtain a high-purity product.

Preservation typically refers to methods for maintaining the stability and shelf life of the final product. It is not a direct component of downstream processing but may be relevant to the overall product lifecycle.
Expression refers to the production of the desired product (e.g., a protein) in a bioprocess. It is generally considered part of upstream processing.

questions.explanation: After the formation of the product in a bioreactor, it undergoes some processes before a finished product to be ready for marketing. Downstream processing includes separation and purification processes. The product obtained is subjected to quality control, testing and kept in suitable preservatives.

questions.text: Which of the following restriction enzymes produces blunt ends?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sal I is a restriction enzyme that produces sticky ends by making staggered cuts in DNA.
Eco RV is a restriction enzyme that produces blunt ends by making straight cuts in DNA.
Xho I is a restriction enzyme that produces sticky ends by making staggered cuts in DNA.
Hind III is a restriction enzyme that produces sticky ends by making staggered cuts in DNA.

questions.explanation: EcoRV is a type II restriction endonuclease isolated from certain strains of E.coli. It creates blunt ends. It recognizes the palindromic sequence of 6 bases as shown here:SalI, XhoI and HindIII restriction enzymes produce sticky ends.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a feature of the plasmids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Some plasmids are transferable, meaning they can be passed from one bacterial cell to another, often through horizontal gene transfer mechanisms like conjugation. This allows for the exchange of genetic material.

Plasmids are usually double-stranded DNA molecules. Single-stranded DNA is less common in the context of plasmids, although there are exceptions like certain filamentous phage plasmids that contain single-stranded DNA.

Plasmids typically have a circular DNA structure. This circular configuration is distinct from the linear structure of the host cell's chromosomal DNA.

In molecular biology, restriction enzymes are used for various applications, including DNA manipulation, genetic engineering, and DNA analysis.

A plasmid is a small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule that is separate from the chromosomal DNA in a cell.
