options.text: Insertional inactivation of alpha galactosidase in recombinant bacteria
options.explanation: Insertional inactivation of alpha-galactosidase in recombinant bacteria involves disrupting or inactivating the gene that encodes the alpha-galactosidase enzyme. This process is commonly used in molecular biology and genetic engineering to create genetically modified bacteria that do not produce or express a specific enzyme, in this case, alpha-galactosidase.

options.text: Inactivation of glycosidase enzyme in recombinant bacteria
options.explanation: Inactivating a glycosidase enzyme in recombinant bacteria involves disrupting or disabling the gene responsible for encoding that specific glycosidase enzyme. Glycosidases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds in carbohydrates. To inactivate such an enzyme in recombinant bacteria, you can follow a process similar to that of insertional inactivation, but tailored to target the specific glycosidase gene.

Non-recombinant bacteria that contain beta-galactosidase are those in which the beta-galactosidase gene is still intact and functional, meaning it has not been disrupted or inactivated by the insertion of a disruption cassette or any other genetic modification. Beta-galactosidase is an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of lactose and other galactosides into glucose and galactose. In the absence of genetic modifications, non-recombinant bacteria that naturally produce beta-galactosidase can utilize lactose as a carbon source.

options.text: Insertional inactivation of alpha galactosidase in non­recombinant bacteria.
options.explanation: Insertional inactivation of alpha-galactosidase in non-recombinant bacteria involves introducing a disruption cassette or a genetic element that inactivates the alpha-galactosidase gene within the non-recombinant bacterial genome. This process results in the loss of the enzyme's function.

questions.explanation: The presence of restriction sites within the markers tet r and amp r of plasmid permits an easy selection for cells transformed with recombinant plasmid. Insertion of the DNA fragment into the plasmid makes antibiotic resistance genes nonfunctional, for example, insertion of the DNA fragment into the plasmid (pBR322) using Pst I or Pvu I makes amp r nonfunctional. Bacterial cells containing such a recombinant pBR322 will be unable to grow in the presence of ampicillin but will grow on tetracycline. This process, however, becomes burdensome because it requires simultaneous plating on two plates having different antibiotics. Thus, an alternative selectable marker is developed to differentiate recombinants and non­ recombinants on the basis of their ability to produce colour in the presence of a chromogenic substance. Here, a recombinant DNA is inserted in the coding sequence of an enzyme B­-galactosidase. pUC 18 plasmid contains this gene which allows it to produce B-galactosidase which degrades certain sugars and produces a blue pigment when exposed to a specific substrate analog. If the plasmid in the bacterium does not have an insert, i.e., is non­ recombinant, the presence of chromogenic substrate gives blue coloured colonies. Presence of insert in the plasmid in recombinant bacterium does not produce any colour, such bacterial colonies are marked as recombinant colonies.

Chitin is a linear polymer of N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. Chitinase breaks down chitin into smaller oligosaccharides or monomeric GlcNAc units. Coeloms are found in several animal groups, including most vertebrates, echinoderms (like sea stars and sea urchins), and annelids (segmented worms).

Coenzymes are organic molecules that are essential for the functioning of enzymes, which are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions in living organisms. Coenzymes are a type of cofactor, which are non-protein molecules or ions that enzymes require to perform their catalytic functions. Coenzymes play a crucial role in various metabolic processes within cells. They are often derived from vitamins and other small organic compounds.

It is a powerful and widely used method for quantifying the concentration of solutes in a sample, identifying substances, and studying the interaction of molecules with light.

Tissue culture, also known as cell culture, is a biological technique in which cells or tissues from a multicellular organism are removed and grown in an artificial environment outside their natural habitat. This artificial environment, often referred to as a "culture," provides the necessary nutrients, growth factors, and conditions to support the growth and replication of these cells or tissues. Tissue culture has a wide range of applications in research, industry, and medicine.

It was developed in the 1980s by Kary Mullis and has since become a fundamental tool in various scientific and medical applications. PCR allows scientists to make millions to billions of copies of a specific DNA sequence from a very small amount of starting material.

Gel electrophoresis is a widely used laboratory technique in molecular biology and biochemistry that separates macromolecules, such as DNA, RNA, and proteins, based on their size and charge. This technique involves applying an electric field to a gel matrix, causing the charged molecules to migrate through the gel. As they move, they become separated based on their size and charge, allowing for the analysis, purification, and quantification of these biomolecules.

This DNA copy is then integrated into the host cell's genome, allowing the virus to replicate within the host cell and potentially remain latent for an extended period. Retroviruses are of significant interest in biology and medicine due to their unique life cycle and their association with various diseases.

options.text: Recognize a specific nucleotide sequence for binding of DNA ligase.

It does so by creating a phosphodiester bond between the phosphate group of one DNA fragment and the deoxyribose sugar of another, effectively sealing the nick or discontinuity in the DNA strand. To perform this ligation, DNA ligase requires a specific nucleotide sequence known as a ligation site or a ligation recognition sequence.

options.text: Restrict the action of the enzyme DNA polymerase.
options.explanation: To restrict the action of the enzyme DNA polymerase, one common method is to use DNA polymerase inhibitors. DNA polymerase inhibitors are chemical compounds or molecules that interfere with the activity of DNA polymerase, preventing or reducing DNA replication.

options.text: Remove nucleotides from the ends of the DNA molecule.
options.explanation: To remove nucleotides from the ends of a DNA molecule, you can use an enzyme known as exonuclease, which cleaves nucleotides one at a time from the ends of a DNA strand. Exonucleases are essential tools in molecular biology and genetic engineering for tasks like DNA sequencing, DNA repair, and DNA modification.

questions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases were found by Arber in 1962 in bacteria. They act as “molecular scissors” or chemical scalpels. options.explanation: Meloidogyne incognita is a species of plant-parasitic nematode, commonly known as the southern root-knot nematode. Nematodes are tiny, unsegmented roundworms that can be found in various environments, including soil. Meloidogyne incognita is a particularly damaging pest in agriculture, as it infects the roots of a wide range of plant species, causing a disease known as "root-knot nematode disease."

Agrobacterium tumefaciens is a Gram-negative bacterium that plays a crucial role in plant biology and genetic engineering. It is a soilborne bacterium that is best known for its ability to transfer a specific segment of its DNA, known as the Ti (tumor-inducing) plasmid, into the genomes of plants. This unique capability to transfer genetic material into plants has made Agrobacterium tumefaciens a valuable tool in biotechnology and genetic engineering.

questions.explanation: Agrobacterium tumefaciens has been extensively used in genetic engineering experiments. It is the causative agent of crown gall, an important disease of many commercial crops. This disease has come to be recognized in recent years as being caused by a DNA plasmid (Ti plasmid) carried by bacterium and transferred to the plant cells. Following the discovery of the relationship between crown gall and the Ti plasmid, this plasmid has come to be widely used in plant genetic engineering as a vector in order to inject a novel gene in a host plant to form a transgenic plant.

options.explanation: DNA polymerase is an enzyme that plays a central role in DNA replication, repair, and synthesis of new DNA strands. It is essential for maintaining the integrity and accuracy of the genetic information in an organism's genome. There are multiple types of DNA polymerases, each with specific functions and properties.

Exonucleases are a class of enzymes that cleave nucleotide sequences from the ends (either the 5' or 3' end) of a DNA or RNA molecule. These enzymes play crucial roles in various cellular processes, including DNA replication, repair, recombination, and RNA degradation. Exonucleases can be classified into two main types: 3' to 5' exonucleases and 5' to 3' exonucleases.

DNA ligase is an essential enzyme in molecular biology that plays a crucial role in the replication, repair, and recombination of DNA molecules. Its primary function is to catalyze the formation of phosphodiester bonds between adjacent nucleotides, sealing breaks or gaps in DNA strands. DNA ligase is instrumental in the final stages of DNA replication, the repair of DNA damage, and the creation of recombinant DNA molecules.

Endonucleases are a class of enzymes that cleave phosphodiester bonds within a DNA or RNA molecule. Unlike exonucleases, which remove nucleotides from the ends of a nucleic acid strand, endonucleases cut the phosphodiester bonds at specific internal sites along the nucleic acid chain. These enzymes play important roles in various cellular processes, including DNA repair, recombination, and the defense against foreign genetic elements like viruses.

questions.explanation: The construction of the first recombinant DNA emerged from the possibility of linking a gene encoding antibiotic resistance with a native plasmid. This plasmid DNA acts as a vector to transfer the piece of DNA attached to it. The linking of the antibiotic resistance gene with the plasmid vector became possible with the enzyme DNA ligase, which acts on cut DNA molecules and joins their ends. This makes a new combination of circular autonomously replicating DNA created in vitro and is known as recombinant DNA.

options.explanation: An "enhancer" typically refers to a specific regulatory DNA sequence that plays a crucial role in the control of gene expression. Enhancers are involved in enhancing the transcription of a gene, meaning they increase the rate at which the gene is transcribed into messenger RNA (mRNA).

A transgene is a gene that has been artificially introduced into the genome of an organism, often from a different species. This process is known as genetic engineering, and it involves the insertion of a specific DNA sequence (the transgene) into the host organism's DNA. A promoter is a region of DNA that plays a crucial role in the initiation of transcription, a process by which the information encoded in a gene is used to synthesize messenger RNA (mRNA). Promoters are essential regulatory elements in gene expression and are found in the upstream region of a gene, just before the gene's coding sequence.

A "reporter" refers to a gene, protein, or other marker that is used to monitor or visualize a biological process, often by producing a detectable signal. Reporters are widely used in research to track the activity of genes, the expression of proteins, or the presence of specific molecules.

questions.explanation: DNA restriction endonuclease is an important class of restriction exonucleases, class II, which cut double­stranded DNA molecules only at sites characterized by a specific nucleotide sequence. This mutualistic association is essential for both the bacteria and the plants. Diplococcus is not a specific genus but a bacterial morphology term that describes bacteria appearing in pairs (diplo- means "double"). Many different bacterial genera can exhibit a diplococcal morphology.

Nitrosomonas is a genus of bacteria that plays a crucial role in the nitrogen cycle, particularly in the process of nitrification. Klebsiella is a genus of bacteria that includes various species, some of which can be pathogenic to humans and animals.

Escherichia is a genus of bacteria commonly known as E. coli. It is a gram-negative, facultative anaerobic bacterium found in the intestines of humans and warm-blooded animals. Agrobacterium is a genus of gram-negative soil bacteria. It is best known for its role in plant transformation and genetic engineering.

questions.explanation: E.coli contains many important standard cloning vectors widely used in gene cloning experiments like pBR322 contains origin of replication (ori). Other cloning vectors like PACYC177, pBR324, PRK 64.6 contain ampicillin resistance gene and they are also found in E.coli. Among higher plants, Ti plasmid of Agrobacterium tumefaciens and Ri plasmid of A. rhizogenes is the best known vector. T­-DNA from Ti or Ri plasmid of Agrobacterium is considered to be a very potential vector for cloning experiments with higher plants.

questions.text: Restriction endonucleases are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Used for in vitro DNA synthesis.
options.explanation: In vitro DNA synthesis, DNA polymerases are essential enzymes used to replicate or amplify DNA sequences outside of a living cell. DNA polymerases catalyze the addition of deoxyribonucleotides to a DNA template strand to create a complementary DNA strand.

options.text: Present in mammalian cells for degradation of DNA.
options.explanation: In mammalian cells, the degradation of DNA is primarily carried out by a group of enzymes known as endonucleases and exonucleases. These enzymes play essential roles in DNA repair, DNA recombination, and the maintenance of genomic stability.

questions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases were found by Arber in 1962 in bacteria. They act as “molecular scissors” or chemical scalpels. They recognize the specific base sequence at palindrome sites in DNA duplex and cut its strands. For example, restriction endonuclease EcoRI found in the colon bacteria E.coli reocgnizes the base sequence GAATTC in DNA duplex and cuts its strands between G and A.

questions.text: Genetic engineering is possible, because?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: We can cut DNA at specific sites by endonucleases like DNase I.
options.explanation: DNase I is an endonuclease that can cleave DNA, but it does so in a relatively non-specific manner. It cleaves double-stranded DNA at specific phosphodiester bonds, but the specific sites it cleaves are not predetermined by a recognition sequence as is the case with restriction endonucleases. Instead, DNase I cleaves DNA where there is a sufficient density of phosphodiester bonds, which typically occurs randomly throughout the DNA molecule.

options.text: Restriction endonucleases purified from bacteria can be used in vitro.
options.explanation: Restriction endonucleases, which are enzymes purified from bacteria, are commonly used in vitro for a wide range of molecular biology and genetic engineering applications. These enzymes are essential tools in molecular biology, allowing researchers to cut DNA at specific recognition sequences and manipulate DNA molecules with a high degree of precision.

options.text: The phenomenon of transduction in bacteria is well understood.
options.explanation: Bacterial transduction is a mechanism of horizontal gene transfer in bacteria, where genetic material is transferred from one bacterium to another through the use of bacteriophages (phages), which are viruses that infect bacteria. Bacterial transduction plays a significant role in bacterial evolution and the spread of genetic information within bacterial populations.

options.text: We can see DNA by electron microscope.

questions.explanation: DNA restriction endonuclease are an important class of restriction exonucleases, class II, which cut double­stranded DNA molecules only at sites characterized by a specific nucleotide sequence. Restriction enzymes are isolated from bacterial cells, and are tools for molecular biologists. Several hundred restriction enzymes are now known, each with a specific sequence requirement dictating where it will cut DNA. Some, such as Hind III, make staggered cuts leaving ‘sticky ends’, three nucleotides long protruding on one strand from each severed terminus; others make clean cuts in both strands at the same place and thus generate ‘blunt ends’. Digesting DNA with a restriction enzyme therefore creates a characteristic set of fragments, which can be isolated by electrophoresis and subsequently analyzed.

questions.text: Which of the following organelles is related with genetic engineering?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles found in the cells of most eukaryotic organisms, including animals, plants, and fungi. Often referred to as the "powerhouses of the cell," mitochondria play a critical role in cellular energy production.

Plasmids are small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecules that are separate from the chromosomal DNA in bacterial cells and some other types of cells. They are commonly found in prokaryotes, such as bacteria, but can also be engineered for use in eukaryotic cells. Plasmids have several important characteristics and are widely used in molecular biology and biotechnology.

The Golgi apparatus, often referred to as the Golgi complex or Golgi body, is a cellular organelle found in eukaryotic cells. It plays a crucial role in processing, modifying, sorting, and packaging macromolecules, particularly proteins and lipids, for transport to their final destinations within or outside the cell.

Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles found in eukaryotic cells, and they play a critical role in intracellular digestion and cellular waste management. They are often referred to as the "garbage disposals" or "suicide sacs" of the cell due to their role in breaking down and recycling cellular components and materials.

questions.explanation: Plasmids are an extrachromosomal genetic element found in many bacteria and in a few eukaryotic cells. Plasmids are closed circles of double­ stranded DNA, ranging in size from one to 200 kilobases. They frequently carry genes conferring antibiotic resistance. Plasmids are becoming important tools for genetic engineering since they have the ability to replicate and migrate to daughter cells. Plasmids are widely used as carriers of cloned genes, as for example the E.coli plasmid pBR322. When plasmids are used as cloning vectors and carry a novel DNA sequence they are referred to as chimeric plasmids.

questions.text: Homozygous pure lines in cattle can be obtained by?

options.text: Mating of unrelated individuals of same breed.
options.explanation: Mating of unrelated individuals of the same breed is a common breeding practice in animal husbandry and agriculture, and it is often referred to as "outbreeding." This approach involves pairing animals that are not closely related but belong to the same breed or population. Outbreeding is used to introduce genetic diversity into a breeding program and has several potential advantages.

options.text: Mating of individuals of different breed
options.explanation: Mating individuals of different breeds is known as "crossbreeding" or "interbreeding." Crossbreeding involves the mating of animals from two distinct breeds or populations. This breeding strategy is used in animal husbandry and agriculture for various reasons, and it can have several advantages and potential drawbacks: Mating individuals of different breeds is known as "crossbreeding" or "interbreeding." Crossbreeding involves the mating of animals from two distinct breeds or populations. This breeding strategy is used in animal husbandry and agriculture for various reasons, and it can have several advantages and potential drawbacks.

options.text: Mating of individuals of different species.
options.explanation: Mating of individuals of different species, often referred to as "interspecies mating" or "hybridization," is a biological process that occurs when two individuals from distinct species produce offspring together. While it is relatively common in the plant kingdom, interspecies mating is much less common in the animal kingdom and can result in a variety of outcomes, depending on the species involved.

options.text: Mating of related individuals of same breed.
options.explanation: Mating related individuals of the same breed is a breeding practice known as "inbreeding." Inbreeding involves the mating of individuals that share a common ancestry or have a close genetic relationship within a specific breed or population. While inbreeding can lead to certain advantages, it also comes with potential disadvantages.

questions.explanation: When breeding is between animals of the same breed for 4­-6 generation, it is called inbreeding. Inbreeding, as a rule, increases homozygosity. Thus, inbreeding is necessary if we want to obtain a pure line in any animal.

options.text: Animals within same breed without having common ancestors
Superior males and females of different breeds.
More closely related individuals within same breed for 4-6 generations.

questions.explanation: In interspecific hybridization, a species is mated with a different related species of the same genus. Interspecific hybrids are generally difficult to produce, but they are important in plant breeding, particularly in breeding for disease resistance. This is also called intrageneric hybridization.

questions.text: Among the following edible fishes, which one is a marine fish having a rich source of omega­-3 fatty acids?

options.explanation: Mystus is a genus of freshwater catfish belonging to the family Bagridae. These catfish are commonly known as "butterfly catfishes" or "whiptail catfishes." The Mystus genus includes various species of small to medium-sized catfish found in freshwater habitats across Asia, particularly in South and Southeast Asia.

"Mangur" typically refers to a species of catfish known as the Asian catfish (Clarias batrachus) or the walking catfish. This species is native to South and Southeast Asia and is known for its ability to survive in oxygen-poor or stagnant waters and even tolerate short periods out of the water. Due to its hardy nature, the walking catfish has been introduced to various regions outside its native range, leading to concerns as an invasive species in some areas.

It is an economically important fish and is widely cultivated for its meat.

Mackerel refers to a group of saltwater fish species belonging to the family Scombridae. These fish are known for their distinctive flavor, rich oil content, and versatility in culinary preparations. questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Strip cropping is a method of farming which involves cultivating a field partitioned into long, narrow strips which are alternated in a crop rotation system. It is used when a slope is too steep or when there is no alternative method of preventing soil erosion.

In shifting agriculture a plot of land is cleared and cultivated for a short period of time; then it is abandoned and allowed to revert to its natural vegetation while the cultivator moves on to another plot.

Ley farming; ley system cultivation. Definition: The term "ley" is derived from the old-English "lea" which means a grassy fallow vegetation on former crop fields. It refers to a land use system where arable crops alternate with vegetation used for livestock production.

Description. Contour farming is the practice of tillage, planting, and other farming operations performed on or near the contour of the field slope. This method is most effective on slopes between two (2) and ten (10) percent.

questions.explanation: Ley farming is an agricultural system where the field is alternately seeded for grain and left fallow for growing hay or used for pasture. During the fallow/pasture period the soil is filled with roots of grasses and other plants. New ploughing mixes them in the soil and also increases the amount of nitrogen in the soil especially when legume forage are used. It also protects soil from erosion by maintaining constant soil coverage.

options.explanation: Infectious coryza is a well-recognized and commonly encountered upper respiratory tract disease of chickens that is caused by the bacterium Haemophilus paragallinarum. The occurrence of recent outbreaks in North America has emphasized that the disease can be significant in meat chickens as well as layer chickens.

Newcastle disease is a highly contagious disease of birds caused by a para-myxo virus. Birds affected by this disease are fowls, turkeys, geese, ducks, pheasants, partridges, guinea fowl and other wild and captive birds, including ratites such ostriches, emus and rhea

Pasteurellosis is a bacterial infection caused by Pasteurella bacteria. Pasteurella multocida is the species which most commonly infects humans. Pasteurella multocida can also infect cattle, rabbits, cats and dogs. Pasteurella infection in cattle is an opportunistic infection.

Salmonella infection is usually caused by eating raw or undercooked meat, poultry, and eggs or egg products or by drinking unpasteurized milk. The incubation period — the time between exposure and illness — can be 6 hours to 6 days. Often, people who have salmonella infection think they have the stomach flu.

questions.explanation: New castle disease is a highly contagious zoonoic bird disease affecting many domestic and wild avian species.The causal agent, New castle disease virus (NDV), is a negative­sense single­ stranded RNA virus. Transmission occurs by exposure to faecal and other excretions from infected birds, and through contact with contaminated feed, water, equipment and clothing. Signs of infection with NDV vary greatly depending on factors such as the strain of virus and the health, age and species of the host. They can include respiratory signs (gasping, coughing), nervous signs (depression, inappetence, drooping wings, paralysis), swelling of the eyes and neck, diarrhoea, rough or thin­shelled aggs and reduced egg production.

questions.explanation: A gene bank or genomic library is a complete collection of cloned DNA fragments that comprises the entire genome of an organism. Molecular probes are small DNA segments that are used to detect the presence of complementary sequences in nucleic acid samples in the genomic library. These are usually formed of 200­500 nucleotide sequences. These segments or probes are labeled either with radioactive or nonradioactive compounds. Probes with DNA sequences complementary to the gene to be isolated are used. They bind with the desired gene, making it visible and help in isolating it from the library.

The deficiency of Adenosine deaminase (ADA) causes immunodeficiency. ADA is an enzyme that is responsible for breaking down adenosine, a type of nucleic acid. When ADA is deficient, adenosine builds up in the body, which can damage the immune system. This can lead to a variety of immunodeficiency disorders, including severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID).
